did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including VGG16, ResNet50, and an ensemble of both, to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. It employed prediction difference analysis with conditional sampling to interpret feature importance and identify biomarkers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training of deep learning models (VGG16, ResNet50, and an ensemble) on the ISIC 2018 dataset to classify skin lesions. It includes a specific methodology for feature interpretation using prediction difference analysis, reports quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 scores) in tables, and validates findings with dermatologists. The structure includes Abstract, Introduction, Method, Experiments and Results, and Conclusion, which is characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: From Deep Learning Towards Finding Skin Lesion Biomarkers
journal: 2019 IEEE 16th International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of accurately classifying skin lesions, particularly melanoma, by using deep learning to identify interpretable biomarkers in dermoscopy images. The authors employed Convolutional Neural Networks, specifically VGG16, ResNet50, and an ensemble of both, trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset containing over 10,000 images across seven lesion classes. To interpret model decisions, they utilized prediction difference analysis with conditional sampling to generate weight of evidence maps highlighting feature importance. The ensemble model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.85, outperforming individual models, and successfully identified clinically relevant features such as pigment networks and blue-white veils. Validation by dermatologists confirmed that the discovered biomarkers aligned with established clinical criteria, including the surprising finding that surrounding skin context contributes to classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification and biomarker discovery. 1113 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes blue-white veils and dark brown homogeneous areas.
Benign lesion class. 6705 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes pigment network and globular structures.
Skin cancer class. 514 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes small gathered spots/blue-gray globules.
Pre-cancerous/cancerous lesion class. 327 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes annular-granular structures and hair follicle openings surrounded by a white halo.
Benign lesion class. 1099 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes small nub-like extensions.
Benign lesion class. 115 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes peripheral delicate pigment network.
Benign lesion class. 142 samples in the ISIC 2018 dataset. Clinical evidence includes circumscribed and ovoid structures (lacunae of blood vessels).

all_task_types.target: Seven skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a 7-class classification task using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. Models (VGG16, ResNet50, and an ensemble) predict discrete diagnostic categories. The primary target is classifying skin lesions into specific diagnostic groups. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores for each class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 (Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection)
datasets_used.description: An open challenge dataset collected from leading clinical centers internationally and acquired from a variety of devices. It consists of RGB color dermoscopy images with size 450x600.

use.training: 70% of the data was used as the training set, with augmentation methods including random rotation, flipping, and zoom-in cropping.
use.validation: 10% of the data was used as the validation set to find the early stopping epoch.
use.testing: 20% of the data was used as the testing set to evaluate the algorithms. The testing set contained 2005 samples.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To interpret feature importance and identify biomarkers by measuring the change in classification probability when specific regions of interest (ROI) in the input image are corrupted.
To quantify the relevance of specific image features to the classification decision by calculating the log-odds difference between the original image and the corrupted image.
Used within the prediction difference analysis to marginalize out the corrupted ROI and estimate the class probability of the corrupted image.
To verify that the biomarkers discovered by the deep learning model align with established clinical criteria and expert opinion.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores, and feature importance was assessed using Weight of Evidence (WE) derived from prediction difference analysis, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

ISIC 2018.notes: The ensemble model (VGG16 + ResNet50) outperformed the single models. The interpretation analysis was applied to the ensemble model. Win size of 15 was chosen for prediction difference analysis as it captured most distinguishable features. Dermatologist validation confirmed that identified biomarkers (e.g., blue-white veils for MEL, pigment network for NV) aligned with clinical criteria. Surrounding skin context was found to contribute to classification.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by references to successful image classification CNNs and ImageNet challenges, though not explicitly stated as 'ImageNet pre-trained' in the text, standard practice for VGG/ResNet is assumed; however, strictly based on text: 'modified and fine-tuned two successful image classification CNNs architectures: ResNet50 [11] and VGG [12]').

architecture_details.modifications: Fully connected (FC) layers modified by removing the last FC layer and adding an FC layer with 128 kernels followed by an FC layer with 7 kernels (for 7-class classification).
architecture_details.ensemble_method: LightGBM (boosting tree-based learning algorithm) used to combine features from different CNN models.

classification_or_output_head.details: FC layer with 7 kernels for 7-class skin lesions classification

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation on the training set including random rotation (up to 25 degrees), left-right flipping, top-bottom flipping, and zoom-in cropping (ratio 0.8)
Laplace correction applied to probability estimates to avoid zero probabilities
conclusions: The proposed pipeline using an ensemble of VGG16 and ResNet50 deep learning models effectively classifies skin lesions, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.85 on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
Prediction difference analysis with conditional sampling successfully identified interpretable biomarkers (saliency features) that align with established clinical criteria verified by dermatologists.
For melanoma, the model identified clinically relevant features such as blue-white veils and dark brown homogeneous areas lacking pigment network.
For nevi, the model identified pigment networks as key features.
The study discovered that surrounding skin context contributes to the classification decision, a finding not included in traditional diagnosis rules but validated by dermatologists.
The method demonstrates the potential for deep learning to assist in identifying new or confirming existing biomarkers for skin lesion diagnosis.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding limited and unbalanced training data and propose future work to integrate segmentation probability maps and explore other interpretation methods like LIME, DeepLift, and Integrated Gradients.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which, while collected from leading clinical centers, contains significantly unbalanced class distributions (e.g., 6705 Nevus vs. 115 Dermatofibroma), potentially biasing model performance.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the held-out test set of the ISIC 2018 dataset, limiting generalizability assessment.

The interpretation method (Prediction Difference Analysis) uses a fixed window size (win size = 15) to corrupt regions; the paper notes this was chosen empirically to capture distinguishable features, which may not be optimal for all lesion types.
The ensemble model (VGG16 + ResNet50) achieved an overall accuracy of 0.85, but melanoma recall was relatively low (0.54), indicating a risk of false negatives in clinical deployment.
The paper acknowledges that the training data is limited and unbalanced, which is a known limitation for deep learning models in medical imaging.
Feature interpretation was validated by only two dermatologists from different institutions, which may not provide robust clinical consensus or broad expert agreement.

The study found that surrounding skin context contributes to classification, a finding not included in traditional diagnosis rules (like ABCD), which challenges standard clinical criteria but requires further validation.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for the performance differences between models or the biomarker identification results.
The method assumes that the most accurate classifier provides the most reliable interpreted results, which is a theoretical assumption not empirically proven in the study.
